author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: While I agree with you in the general idea that science is a belief, it is more than that because we use that belief to test future belief predictions. In so doing, we confirm that when we do a certain thing X then Y happens, to some higher degree of certainty. So, yes... science often has theories and there are different ways of applying those theories... and one could say that we never "prove" anything with science. But, we certainly improve our predictive ability as we improve our theories.  It is possible then there could be other causes for Y, and certainly we can't be sure without some measure of infinity that doing X will "always" result in Y, but to the best of our knowledge, that is what happens. Generally tho, it is rare for a paradigm shift to completely negate existing theories, tho it does happen. If we look at the past, we see that older theories often made predictions that were incorrect, and therefore we needed to find better theories that made the correct predictions. In that way, science tends to improve on itself... IF the proper methodology is followed. Certainly, science can and will fail when people ignore the evidence and hold to a theory dogmatically in the face of evidence. Additionally, we must also understand that science doesn't necessitate that we actually know that what we theorize is correct. There are often many reasons for something to happen and many complex interactions that we cannot hope to accurately predict and yet we claim we can. Our biggest issues with science seem to be our folly that we over believe theories for complex systems without checking whether or not those predictions matched past events. Add in the complex interplay between science and the forces around the incentives and disincentives... and we also have issues to contend with to keep the science on the right track.

author: @frankbenevolence
text: I think you are underestimating the competitive aspect of science.  The math or experiment can be checked by your peers,  by this process the truth will be born out by the  data.  Einstein being the  prime example, no one in the science community wanted him to be right but they couldn't disprove him so his ideas they became canon.  Religion has no data and is 100 % arbitrary

replies.author: @TnTtalktime
replies.text: What do you mean by peers? If you mean a group of people of equal standing then you are already in trouble as 72 percent of the worlds population believe in a god of some form or other (Gallop survey 2022) - So, I can only think you mean a group of scientists of equal standing. In which case it doesn't mean much if a group of people who already believe in something agree you are right if you perpetuate their belief. You must know that data will very very often support two completely opposed interpretations?

replies.author: @frankbenevolence
replies.text:
@TnTtalktime I mean that no one in the religious community is rewriting their holy books in an attempt to match with reality.  Religious claims are not scrutinized for accuracy and are often made despite evidence not because of it.  Religion requires the active maintenance of wilful ignorance of the world and its mechanisms in a way Science does not.  Faith based decisions are definitionally without evidence.
In the science community any new claims have to explain the existing data better than existing theories. If it can do that then it will be accepted, if it is contradicted by existing data or makes claims not indicated by the data then it isn't taken seriously.  Any extraordinary claims need to be accompanied by extraordinary evidence.  
It isn't "just believe in science" as a declaration , it gives you the data and encourages you to question it (and gives prizes and fame to people who do successfully).  It also uses  multiple lines of inquiry, geologists and biologists and chemists and physicists all end up with the converging explanations from completely different directions.
Science telling a cohesive, self consistent and evidence based story of the world is why I give it more credence.  I understand that it requires that you trust there isn't a world wide conspiracy in every country and across every field of study but that is both laughably unlikely and wouldn't explain how science makes accurate predictions.  Some scientists become dogmatic in their beliefs, but that is a breach of scientific principles not the basis of them as it is with Religious beliefs.
For example the belief that the earth is round is not on equal footing with the belief that it is flat because one is supported by literally all the evidence, and the other is maintained despite disproving it being relatively easy.

replies.author: @frankbenevolence
replies.text: @TnTtalktime by peers I mean people who study in the same field re-running your experiments to see if they can be replicated.  Newton's belief in numerology and alchemy is not accepted by the scientific community despite Newton being a brilliant scientist, in the same way as Kepler being Christian doesn't vindicate Christianity.

author: @mhegyi2
text:
Blind faith, or religious faith, is the belief in something because of lack of evidence, or in spite of contrary evidence. Reasonable expectations based previous experience is not faith.

Religion starts with a conclusion, then cherry picks or invents evidence to support said conclusion. All contrary evidence is just their antagonist testing your “faith”. Science starts with evidence, comes to a tentative conclusion, then tries to figure out why it’s wrong. It ends each conclusion with “wrong” or “not wrong yet”, remaining open to new evidence.

The reason why people think scientists have faith like religion is people not in that particular field of study are unaware of just how much evidence supports the currently accepted theory. Paradigm shifts are hard to do for good reason.

Lack of language and mixing in colloquial terms cause some of this confusion. We use faith to describe blind faith, trust, and label religious organizations as “a faith”.

author: @Ken00001010
text:
When someone tells me that I am also going on faith or belief, I respond, "No, I have reasonable expectations based on prior evidence." Now it is true that some of the people take science on faith, but many are going on the results they see around them (prior evidence) of what has worked. Any amount of faith in prayer has not brought rain or stopped floods or healed the sick the way application of scientific knowledge has. The collection of facts moves science along, but the real job of science is to build a model of the world that matches and explains the facts. The best models also predict situations (promote expectations) that have not been seen, that then later are tested and found to match those predictions.

Yes, science is done by fallible humans, and we evolved to make up stories that had both things that worked and did not. Perhaps soon there will be Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) doing science for us, but at this point we are still going on the best we can. But, a big difference with religion is that when two sides disagree, they don't settle it by which can go inflect genocide on the other, they have to present evidence based on measured facts. Also, Newton lived long before the formalization of scientific research, and someone as madd as.a bag of kittens about to be thrown into a canal would not have a scientific job, today. Newton as a person is not a model for scientists, today. Don't blame science (the search for knowledge) for the bad behavior of individual human scientists.

The idea that you cannot go against the scientific consensus is true when you have no evidence to back up your position. Day in and day out scientists get mail from crackpots who think they have discovered the "suppressed truth." There is a good reason it is not easy to introduce new theory, it takes evidence. In some cases you do have to wait for your teachers to die to make an advance, but that is not the general case. Also, today a scientific paper gets out on the Web, and if it has the goods, publication suppression is impossible.

Karl Popper did not state that something is true until you can show that it is false. He stated that all we know is provisional until it is shown to be false. Inductive reasoning is where you get the priors for good expectations. Yes, you never get 100% certainty about a future event, but all of technology is based on having a high enough probability to expect stuff to work.

Science is not belief, it is reasonable expectation.

replies.author: @andyjones1982
replies.text: Expectation is belief. Reasonable expectation is just reasonable belief.

replies.author: @Ken00001010
replies.text: @andyjones1982 Expectation is actually a technical term for the probability of an event times the result of that event. Expectation based on prior evidence is a form of Bayesian reasoning.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @andyjones1982 Yes, but you use reasonable belief everyday... otherwise you would not worry about stopping at red lights, and all that stuff related to safety. We don't call that "science" but, that is really the essence of it... that we have a reasonable belief that doing x will result in y. We know that if we don't stop at a stop light, there is a reasonable chance we could get hit by a car coming from another direction. It doesn't always happen, but the chances are higher. Essentially, science is a very much like a method for improving the predictive power at our command.

Truth... Things can be partially true.  

Is it acceptable to claim something as true when yiu cannot demonstrate it is true?

I like this definition for truth:

"True" is the extent to which a proposition conforms to reality, as measured by predictive power.

From this I can identify things that are true based on testing, and can eliminate the need to consider things that are not defined in a way that can be tested.

author: @JohnBoen
text:
Interesting topic for me - I see this discussion a lot. Great topic.

And so many people I admire describe these in different ways.  We don’t all use the terms the same way.

Like trust vs faith: 
Faith and trust are based on different concepts. They are not interchangeable words for me.
* We trust someone/something/some-process because we have a history with it. It is a conclusion based on limited evidence.

* we have faith in something when the outcome is purely speculative and there is no rstional way to predict the odds.

A lot of people do not make this distinction.

text: There's also the minor inconvenience that people prefer to have jobs & therefore will engineer results to keep their jobs. My personal response is to ask my self what is the gain to the person giving the information & then compare it with what I think/know looking for incontinences & then compare with other sources of information. If I tell someone something i believe is true i almost always tell them to look it up themselves on a number of different locations to test my statement & I love it when I get feedback especially if i learn something.

replies.author: @johnbash-on-ger
replies.text: The solution to that is having social safety nets. And with the ever increasing capabilities of automation, the general population will eventually be mostly be NEETs on NEETbux. NEET, an acronym for "Not in Education, Employment, or Training", refers to a person who is unemployed and not receiving an education or vocational training. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NEET

author: @davidprocter3578
text: Science is shackled by the foibles of the human mind.

author: @JohnSmith-qm5xu
text: Science has been demonstrated to be true. Religion has not. Trust us not faith. Science is *not* marketing. You threw in “used in selling cars and toothpaste” that’s not science, that’s cherry picking points and it’s called marketing. Science doesn’t string ppl up on lamp posts either, that’s RELIGION. Scientific community just laugh at you and move on.
